title: Shopping Cart & Order Management — System Design
description: Designing the cart-to-order path of an e-commerce platform: hold items in a cart, price them with promotions, then turn checkout into a durable order that coordinates payment, inventory, and fulfillment without losing money or overselling. The interesting problems are correctness under failure: a cart that survives sessions, pricing computed at the right moment, and a checkout that spans several independent services (a distributed transaction it can't truly have). We build from a naive cart toward a saga-based, idempotent, inventory-consistent order system.

nodes.label: Commerce API
nodes.description: Commerce API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.description: Client: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Cart Service
nodes.description: Cart Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Cart Store
nodes.description: Cart Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Catalog / Products
nodes.description: Catalog / Products: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Checkout Orchestrator
nodes.description: Checkout Orchestrator: Workflow component that coordinates multi-step operations or compensation.

nodes.label: Shopper App
nodes.description: Shopper App: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Fulfillment Service
nodes.description: Fulfillment Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Inventory
nodes.description: Inventory: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Idempotency Store
nodes.description: Idempotency Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Inventory Service
nodes.description: Inventory Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Inventory DB
nodes.description: Inventory DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Checkout
nodes.description: Checkout: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Checkout 1
nodes.description: Checkout 1: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Checkout 2
nodes.description: Checkout 2: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Order
nodes.description: Order: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Order Service
nodes.description: Order Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Orders DB
nodes.description: Orders DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Outbox
nodes.description: Outbox: Append-only event stream, feed, bus, or log used for ordering, replay, or fan-out.

nodes.label: Payment
nodes.description: Payment: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Payment System
nodes.description: Payment System: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Pricing & Promotions
nodes.description: Pricing & Promotions: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Promotion Rules
nodes.description: Promotion Rules: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Order Event Queue
nodes.description: Order Event Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Shard Router
nodes.description: Shard Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Cart Shard A
nodes.description: Cart Shard A: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Cart Shard B
nodes.description: Cart Shard B: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

functional: Add, update, and remove items in a cart.
Persist the cart across sessions and devices for a logged-in user.
Price the cart with taxes and promotions/coupons.
Check out: reserve inventory, take payment, and create an order.
Track order lifecycle (placed, paid, fulfilled, cancelled) and allow cancellation.
nonFunctional: Never oversell limited inventory; never leave a charge without a recoverable order or compensation record.
Idempotent checkout so retries don't double-order or double-charge.
Cart operations are fast and highly available (browsing must feel instant).
Order processing is durable and recoverable across service failures.
Pricing shown at checkout is honored (no surprise changes mid-flow).
Scale carts (huge, read/write heavy) independently from orders (fewer, correctness-critical).

capacity.label: Cart operations
capacity.note: Add/update/view; latency-sensitive, favoring availability over strict consistency. ~2KB/cart x 300M active carts ≈ 600GB working set — enough to spread across a sharded, RAM-class KV store.

capacity.label: Checkouts
capacity.note: ~25x rarer than cart ops but correctness-critical. Each writes 1 order + N reservations + 1 outbox event ≈ 5-10 CP writes, so ~10-20k order-side writes/s at peak.

capacity.label: Active carts
capacity.note: Most never convert; a ~7-day TTL bounds storage and expires abandoned carts cheaply.

capacity.label: Inventory contention
capacity.note: Flash-sale items concentrate writes on one row; CAS holds keep it lock-free, but a hot SKU may need admission control / sharded counters.

capacity.label: Idempotency + outbox
capacity.note: ~2k keys/s x 72h ≈ 0.5B rows to retain then prune; outbox throughput tracks checkout + state-transition rate (~5-10k events/s).

capacity.label: Order durability
capacity.note: Synchronously-replicated CP order DB; single-writer region for orders/checkout with active-active AP carts, outbox replayed after failover.

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Two very different workloads — split them.

api.description: Add or update a cart line item. Cart is scoped to the authenticated user/session (ownership enforced server-side — no IDOR). Highly available; eventual consistency is fine. Concurrent multi-device edits reconcile by last-write-wins per line (or a cart version), so confirmed mutations are not silently lost.
Fetch the cart with current priced totals.

Convert a cart to an order: reserve inventory, create a PENDING_PAYMENT order, authorize/capture payment, mark PAID. Idempotency-Key required; a repeat with the same key returns the original result, a repeat with a different payload is rejected 422, and a still-running attempt returns 409 in_progress.
api.response: 201 Created { "order_id": "o_5", "status": "paid" } — or 409 if a checkout for this key is still in_progress, or 409 if an item is out of stock

messages.label: create order PENDING_PAYMENT (frozen price, reservation ids)
mark PAID + confirm holds (emit order.paid via outbox)

api.description: Cancel an order; compensates per its current state — pending_payment releases holds and voids any auth, paid (not fulfilled) voids/refunds and releases, fulfilled routes to a return/refund flow. Rejected (409) if the order is in a non-cancellable state.
api.response: 200 OK { "status": "cancelled" } — or 409 if the order can no longer be cancelled

dataModel.name: carts
dataModel.note: A user's working set of items. Cheap, expiring, availability-first.

fields.name: items
fields.type: jsonb (sku, qty, updated_at per line for LWW merge)

dataModel.name: orders
dataModel.note: The durable record of an order and its lifecycle state. Created PENDING_PAYMENT before capture so a charge always has a recoverable record. Line items snapshot the frozen price.

fields.name: items
fields.type: jsonb (sku, qty, unit_price, currency, discount, tax, promo_ids, coupon_code)

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(pending_payment, paid, fulfillment_requested, fulfilled, cancel_requested, cancelled, failed)

dataModel.name: order_events
dataModel.note: Append-only history of order state transitions for audit and recovery; one row per legal transition.

dataModel.name: outbox_events
dataModel.note: Events written in the same transaction as the order state change; a relay publishes them to the queue exactly once relative to the commit. Consumers dedupe on event_id.

dataModel.name: price_quotes
dataModel.note: An immutable, server-computed price snapshot for a cart, frozen at checkout and referenced by orders.price_quote_id. Captures the rule versions used so an audit can reproduce the total, and a short expiry bounds how long a quote can back a checkout.

dataModel.name: coupon_redemptions
dataModel.note: Authoritative ledger that enforces per-coupon and per-user usage limits. Unique on (coupon_code, order_id) so a retried checkout reuses its redemption; the Promotion service owns redemption idempotency and limit checks at checkout. If a checkout fails, its redemption is released so the limit isn't consumed.

dataModel.name: inventory
dataModel.note: Authoritative stock per SKU. The invariant available + reserved + sold = stock_on_hand holds at all times: a hold moves a unit available -> reserved (CAS), confirmation moves reserved -> sold, release/expiry moves reserved -> available. stock_on_hand only changes on receiving/adjustment; sold is reconciled against immutable paid order lines.

fields.name: stock_on_hand
fields.type: int (physical units; changes only on receiving/adjustment)

fields.name: sold
fields.type: int (confirmed; reconciled against immutable paid order lines)

dataModel.name: reservations
dataModel.note: Short-lived holds on inventory created at checkout; confirmed on payment, released on failure/expiry. Unique on (checkout_id, sku) so a retried checkout reuses its hold instead of double-reserving.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Dedupes retried checkouts; stores the first result. Retained for a bounded window (e.g. 24–72h). A repeat with the same key + matching request_hash replays the stored response; a mismatching hash is rejected (422). Terminal failures (out of stock, validation error, permanent payment decline) are also stored as completed with their error response and a null order_id, so a retry replays the failure instead of re-running the saga.

fields.name: order_id
fields.type: uuid null (null when the checkout completed without creating an order — e.g. out of stock)

fields.name: outcome
fields.type: enum(success, out_of_stock, validation_failed, payment_failed) null (set when completed)

fields.name: response
fields.type: jsonb (stored success or error response replayed on retry)

dataModel.name: payment_attempts
dataModel.note: Durable record of every authorize/capture/void/refund attempt against the gateway, so a retry or lost response reconciles against the attempt rather than re-charging. One row per attempt; the latest row carries the gateway intent and response.

fields.name: idempotency_key
fields.type: varchar (sent to gateway; derived from order id)

dataModel.name: payment_webhook_events
dataModel.note: Dedupe log of inbound gateway webhooks, keyed by the gateway's event id, so a redelivered payment_intent.succeeded is processed exactly once. The reconciler also reads this to advance stale PENDING_PAYMENT orders.

patterns.name: Session vs persistent cart
patterns.what: Keep carts in a fast store keyed by user/session; merge guest carts on login.

patterns.name: Pricing at the right moment
patterns.what: Compute promotions/taxes at view and re-validate (and freeze) at checkout.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever prices/promotions change and must be honored consistently.

patterns.name: Saga / orchestration
patterns.what: Coordinate checkout across inventory, payment, and order via explicit steps with compensations.
patterns.whenToUse: A 'transaction' spanning independent services that can't share one ACID transaction.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: Dedupe retried checkouts so a shopper isn't double-ordered or double-charged.
patterns.whenToUse: Money-moving operations over an unreliable network.

patterns.name: Inventory reservation + TTL
patterns.what: Hold stock during checkout; release if it doesn't complete in time.
patterns.whenToUse: Preventing oversell while a checkout is in flight.

patterns.name: Optimistic concurrency (CAS)
patterns.what: Decrement-if-available on stock so concurrent checkouts can't oversell.

patterns.name: Order state machine
patterns.what: Model the order as explicit states with guarded transitions and compensations.
patterns.whenToUse: Long-running order lifecycle across placement, payment, fulfillment, cancellation.

patterns.name: Transactional outbox
patterns.what: Emit order events atomically with the state change for fulfillment/notifications.
patterns.whenToUse: Reliable downstream events without dual writes.

patterns.name: Workload partitioning
patterns.what: Scale carts and orders as separate systems with different consistency needs.
patterns.whenToUse: When two sub-workloads have opposite priorities (availability vs correctness).

steps.title: 1. Naive Cart & Buy (the baseline)
steps.description: Start simple: a cart held in memory/session, and a 'buy' that decrements stock and records an order in one request. It works for one shopper; it loses carts on restart and, worse, treats a multi-service checkout (inventory + payment + order) as if it were one atomic step — which it can't be. The worst case isn't a failed request the shopper can retry; it's a crash mid-checkout that moves money or holds stock with no recoverable local record.
steps.decisionPrompt: Checkout must reserve stock, charge a card, and create an order across three services. What goes wrong treating that as one transaction?

concepts.definition: No single ACID transaction spans payment, inventory, and orders.
concepts.whyItMatters: A crash between steps leaves money moved but no order, or stock gone with no charge.

concepts.definition: Carts must survive sessions/restarts for logged-in users.
concepts.whyItMatters: An in-memory cart vanishing loses the shopper's intent.

talkingPoints: Name the two priorities: a durable, available cart and a correct multi-service checkout.
Flag that checkout is a saga, not a transaction.

traps.trap: Treating checkout as one atomic DB transaction across services
traps.why: Payment, inventory, and orders are separate systems; a failure mid-way loses money or stock with no order.
traps.instead: Model checkout as a saga with compensations and idempotency.

bottlenecks.issue: Single-request checkout can't be atomic across services and loses carts on restart.

strong: Spots that checkout spans services and can't be one transaction.
Separates cart durability from checkout correctness.
weak: Assumes a DB transaction covers the whole checkout.

whyNow: The baseline exposes the two core problems: cart persistence and cross-service checkout correctness.

view.caption: The shopper app sends add/buy to the Commerce API, which reads the Catalog / Products, then decrements stock and records the order in the Orders DB — all in one request.

steps.title: 2. Persistent Cart Store
steps.description: Move carts to a fast, highly-available store keyed by user (or session for guests), so they survive across devices and restarts. Carts favor availability over strict consistency, expire when abandoned, and merge a guest cart into the user's account cart on login.
steps.decisionPrompt: A guest fills a cart, then logs in. What happens to their items, and where does the cart live?

concepts.definition: Cart writes should almost always succeed; brief inconsistency is acceptable.
concepts.whyItMatters: A failed 'add to cart' loses a sale; correctness can be reconciled at checkout.

concepts.definition: Combining a session cart with the account cart on login.
concepts.whyItMatters: Preserves intent across the auth boundary.

concepts.definition: Reconciling concurrent edits from multiple devices via last-write-wins per line or a cart version.
concepts.whyItMatters: Confirmed cart mutations shouldn't be silently lost when two devices edit at once.

options.name: Fast KV store (e.g. Redis/Dynamo), TTL, AP-leaning (default)
options.description: Carts live in a fast, highly-available key-value store keyed by user or session, with a TTL that expires abandoned carts and AP-leaning replication.
pros: Very fast, highly available cart ops.
TTL expires abandoned carts cheaply; scales to hundreds of millions.
cons: Eventual consistency across replicas; not the place for authoritative stock/pricing.

view.caption: The Cart Service keeps carts in a fast, highly-available key-value Cart Store keyed by user or session.

options.name: Cart rows in the primary SQL DB
options.description: Carts are stored as rows in the same strongly-consistent primary SQL database that holds orders and inventory.
pros: Strong consistency; one datastore.
cons: High-frequency cart writes contend with order/inventory load on the primary.
Harder to scale to huge cart volume.

view.caption: The Cart Service stores carts as rows in the same strongly-consistent Orders DB that holds orders and inventory.

traps.trap: Storing carts in the same primary DB as orders/inventory
traps.why: Cart write volume contends with correctness-critical order/inventory load.
traps.instead: Use a separate fast, available store for carts; keep the primary for orders.

traps.why: Carts are stale and AP-leaning; stock and price can change before checkout.
traps.instead: Treat cart contents as intent; re-validate price and stock at checkout.

traps.trap: Using a guessable guest cart id as the only ownership check
traps.why: If the cart id is just a path parameter, anyone who guesses or scrapes it can read or edit someone else's cart.
traps.instead: Mint an unguessable guest cart token bound to the session/device, and enforce ownership server-side rather than from the URL.

failureDrills.scenario: A cart-store replica is briefly unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Adding to cart still works; the shopper isn't blocked.
failureDrills.mitigation: AP store with failover: writes are retried and reconciled, and stale replicas are acceptable for reads (checkout revalidates price/stock) — but a confirmed cart mutation should not simply vanish, so writes use last-write-wins-per-line or a cart version.

strong: Separates the cart store from the order DB; treats cart as intent.

recap.before: Carts were volatile and on the primary path.
recap.after: Carts live in a fast, available, expiring store, separate from orders.
recap.newRisk: Cart data is eventually consistent — checkout must re-validate.

whyNow: A durable, scalable cart is the foundation; correctness happens at checkout.

view.caption: The shopper app's add/view calls go through the Commerce API to the Cart Service, which persists carts in the Cart Store.

steps.title: 3. Pricing & Promotions
steps.description: Compute price (taxes, discounts, coupons) by a pricing service that reads promotion rules. Prices are shown live on the cart but re-evaluated and frozen at checkout, so the shopper is charged exactly what they agreed to.
steps.decisionPrompt: A coupon expires between adding to cart and checkout. What price does the shopper pay?

concepts.definition: Lock the computed total into the order at checkout time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents surprise changes and disputes; the agreed price is honored.

deepDives.title: Where pricing happens
points: Show indicative pricing on cart view (cheap, cached promotion rules).

Re-evaluate authoritatively at checkout and freeze the result into an immutable price_quotes snapshot referenced by the order, capturing the promotion and tax rule versions so the total is reproducible in an audit.

Validate coupon eligibility/limits server-side at checkout, not just on display: the Promotion service records a coupon_redemptions row (reserved -> confirmed on payment, released on failure) keyed per user/coupon/order, so usage limits hold under retries.
Because the quote and redemption are keyed to the checkout, a retried checkout reuses them rather than re-pricing or double-consuming a coupon.

traps.trap: Trusting the price the client sends at checkout
traps.why: A tampered client could submit any price.
traps.instead: Recompute price server-side at checkout from current rules; never trust client totals.

traps.why: Promotions/taxes can change before checkout, charging the wrong amount.
traps.instead: Re-evaluate at checkout and freeze the result into the order.

failureDrills.scenario: A promotion is misconfigured to stack infinitely.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It doesn't produce a negative or runaway total.
failureDrills.mitigation: Server-side rule limits/caps and coupon usage checks at checkout.

strong: Recomputes and freezes price server-side at checkout; validates coupons.

recap.after: A pricing service computes totals, re-validated and frozen at checkout.
recap.newRisk: Promotion rules are complex and must be capped/validated server-side.

whyNow: The authoritative total must be settled before money moves in checkout.

view.caption: The Cart Service calls Pricing & Promotions, which applies the Promotion Rules and reads the Catalog / Products to compute the cart total.

options.name: Reprice at checkout (default)
options.description: The cart stores only item references; the pricing service computes taxes, discounts, and coupons fresh at checkout, so the shopper is charged the prices valid at that moment.
pros: The shopper pays the price and promotions valid at checkout, so an expired coupon simply doesn't apply.
No stale prices: catalog and promo changes are reflected at the moment of purchase.
Cart stores only item references, keeping it lightweight and easy to merge.
cons: Displayed cart total can change between adding and checking out, surprising the shopper.
A price drop after add-to-cart is good news; a rise needs a clear 'price changed' message.
Pricing/promotions service is on the checkout hot path and must be fast.

view.caption: At checkout the Cart Service calls Pricing & Promotions, which evaluates the Promotion Rules and Catalog / Products to compute fresh prices.

options.name: Lock price at add-to-cart
options.description: The price, discounts, and coupons are computed once when the item is added and persisted per line item, so the shopper sees that frozen price through checkout.
pros: The shopper sees a stable price from add to checkout — no surprises at the end.
Honors the price/coupon that was advertised when they chose to add the item.
Pricing is computed once, off the checkout hot path.
cons: A coupon that expires (or a price that rises) is still honored, leaking margin.
Long-lived carts hold stale prices indefinitely unless you add an expiry.
Must persist the locked price/promo per line item, fattening the cart.

view.caption: Pricing & Promotions evaluates the Promotion Rules once and persists the locked price per line item into the Cart Store at add time.

options.name: Lock price with short TTL
options.description: The locked add-to-cart price is held for a bounded window (e.g. 15 minutes), after which the line item is repriced against current rules.
pros: Shows a stable price for a bounded window (e.g. 15 min), balancing UX and margin.
Expired coupons are eventually dropped instead of honored forever.
Compromise that avoids both checkout-time surprises and indefinite stale pricing.
cons: Adds TTL bookkeeping and a re-price path when the lock expires mid-session.
The price can still change if the shopper lingers past the window.
More complex than either pure strategy to implement and explain.

view.caption: Pricing & Promotions locks the computed price into the Cart Store with a TTL, after which the line item is repriced.

steps.title: 4. Inventory Reservation & Oversell Prevention
steps.description: Make 'never oversell' a hard invariant. At checkout, a single conditional write creates a short-lived hold only if enough stock remains (atomic CAS), decrementing available and incrementing reserved together; the hold has a TTL and auto-releases if checkout doesn't complete. Payment success confirms the hold as sold; failure or expiry releases it. The invariant is available + reserved + sold = stock_on_hand, and inventory is the authoritative source of truth.
steps.decisionPrompt: The last unit of a hot item is in two shoppers' checkouts at once. How do you ensure only one gets it?

concepts.definition: A temporary hold on stock during checkout that auto-expires.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents oversell while a checkout is in flight without permanently locking stock.

concepts.definition: Atomically reduce stock only if enough remains.
concepts.whyItMatters: Concurrent checkouts can't both claim the last unit.

deepDives.title: The invariant and its counter transitions
points: available + reserved + sold = stock_on_hand is maintained by every state change moving a unit between two counters, never creating or destroying one: a hold moves available -> reserved, confirmation moves reserved -> sold, release/expiry moves reserved -> available.
stock_on_hand only changes on physical events (receiving, damage/adjustment); a checkout never touches it, so the sum is preserved by construction.
sold is the count of confirmed reservations and is reconcilable against immutable paid order lines: a periodic job compares sold against SUM(qty) over paid orders per SKU and flags drift.

A sweeper expires holds past their TTL (reserved -> available) so an abandoned or crashed checkout never strands stock; a reconciler audits available/reserved/sold against reservations and paid order lines, and against the warehouse system of record.

flows.name: Two checkouts race for the last unit
flows.note: Atomic CAS reserves for exactly one; the other gets out-of-stock.

traps.trap: Read stock, then write stock-1 as separate steps
traps.why: Two checkouts both read 1 and both write 0 — oversell.
traps.instead: Atomic decrement-if-available (CAS / conditional update); reject when it would go negative.

traps.trap: Decrementing real stock before payment succeeds
traps.why: Abandoned/failed checkouts permanently shrink available stock.
traps.instead: Reserve (separate from sold) with a TTL; convert to sold on payment, release on failure/expiry.

failureDrills.scenario: Checkout reserves stock then crashes before paying.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The reserved unit returns to available shortly.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reservation TTL expires and releases; the order saga compensates.

failureDrills.scenario: A flash sale drives one hot SKU to thousands of reservation writes/second, melting the single inventory row.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reservations stay correct (no oversell) and the system sheds or queues excess load instead of collapsing.
failureDrills.mitigation: First line is admission control: a per-SKU token bucket / virtual waiting room admits only as many concurrent checkouts as there is stock plus a margin, so the CAS row sees bounded write pressure. If a single row is still the ceiling, split the SKU's stock into N sharded reservation buckets (each its own CAS row) and settle against the total; or pin the hot SKU to a single-writer partition that batches decrements. All options keep the available + reserved + sold invariant — they only change where the CAS contention lands.

strong: Uses atomic CAS reservations with TTL; separates reserved from sold.
Has a first production answer for a hot-SKU flash sale (admission control / sharded reservation buckets).

recap.after: One CAS-protected conditional write creates a TTL hold (available -> reserved), making oversell impossible while checkout is in flight; the invariant available + reserved + sold = stock_on_hand is maintained.
recap.newRisk: Holds must be confirmed (reserved -> sold) on payment and released on failure/expiry — handled by the saga plus a sweeper/reconciler for stale holds.

whyNow: Inventory correctness must be solid before orchestrating the full checkout.

view.caption: The Checkout Orchestrator asks the Inventory Service to reserve, which CAS-decrements available and holds units with a TTL in the Inventory DB.

options.name: CAS-protected hold with TTL (default)
options.description: Checkout issues one conditional write that creates a short-lived hold only if available >= qty, atomically decrementing available and incrementing reserved together. The hold has a TTL and auto-releases on expiry; payment success confirms it (reserved -> sold), failure or expiry releases it (reserved -> available). Combines optimistic CAS for oversell safety with a TTL hold for abandoned-checkout recovery.
pros: The conditional write is atomic, so exactly one of two racers wins the last unit without locking.
Distinguishes available / reserved / sold and auto-releases on TTL, so abandoned checkouts free stock instead of stranding it.
Lock-free and O(1), so it survives the hot-item burst without row-lock contention; the invariant available + reserved + sold = stock_on_hand stays explicit.
cons: Adds a holds table plus an expiry sweeper/reconciler to operate.
Available reads must account for active holds.
A single hot SKU is still a write hotspot to scale; TTL tuning trades shopper grace time against over-protecting stock.

view.caption: The Checkout Orchestrator reserves via the Inventory Service, which runs one CAS conditional write — decrement available, create a TTL hold — in the Inventory + Holds store, so only one racer wins the last unit and abandoned holds auto-release.

options.name: Plain CAS decrement of available (no hold)
options.description: Checkout reserves stock with an atomic compare-and-set decrement of the available count only, with no separate hold record — the unit is taken the moment checkout starts.
pros: Simplest oversell guard: a compare-and-set on the available count, O(1) and lock-free.
The single count is the source of truth — easy to reason about.
No holds table or sweeper to operate.
cons: Decrement-on-checkout strands the unit until something releases it — an abandoned checkout shrinks available with no TTL to recover it.
Can't distinguish 'reserved' from 'sold', so availability is wrong during long checkouts.
Needs reconciliation if the fast counter and durable store diverge.

view.caption: The Checkout Orchestrator reserves via the Inventory Service, which atomically CAS-decrements the available count in the Inventory DB with no separate hold record.

options.name: Pessimistic row lock
options.description: Checkout takes an explicit lock on the SKU row, then reads, decrements, and writes the count under that lock within the transaction.
pros: Locks the SKU row for the transaction, giving the strongest serialization with familiar SQL.
Read-modify-write under an explicit lock is easy to reason about.
Guarantees no concurrent mutation of the count.
cons: All buyers of a hot item serialize on one lock, collapsing throughput exactly when it matters.
Lock waits cause timeouts and retry storms during a hot drop.
Same guarantee as a CAS decrement but far worse under contention.

view.caption: The Checkout Orchestrator reserves via the Inventory Service, which takes a SELECT FOR UPDATE lock on the SKU row in the Inventory DB before decrementing.

steps.title: 5. Checkout as a Saga (idempotent)
steps.description: Orchestrate checkout as a saga: reserve inventory -> create a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order (frozen price + reservation ids) -> authorize/capture payment using the order id as the gateway idempotency key -> mark the order PAID and confirm the holds, with compensations on failure (void/refund, release reservation, mark the order FAILED). Creating the order before money moves means there is always a durable local record to reconcile against, and an idempotency key makes the whole flow safe to retry so a shopper is never double-charged or double-ordered.
steps.decisionPrompt: Payment succeeds but order creation fails. What happens to the money and the reserved stock?

concepts.definition: A coordinator runs each step and triggers compensations if a later step fails.
concepts.whyItMatters: Gives cross-service 'all-or-nothing' behavior without a distributed transaction.

concepts.definition: Undo actions (void/refund, release reservation, mark order FAILED) for completed steps when the saga aborts.
Authorization reserves funds on the card; capture actually moves them. Void cancels an uncaptured auth; refund reverses a capture.
concepts.whyItMatters: Separating them lets the saga hold money tentatively and release it cheaply (void) when a later step fails, instead of always refunding.

deepDives.title: Payment lifecycle and reconciliation
points: Order before money: create a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order with the frozen price and reservation ids, then authorize/capture — so a capture always has a local record to reconcile against.

Each gateway call writes a durable payment_attempts row (gateway intent id, idempotency key, amount, kind, status, last response), so a retry or lost response reconciles against the attempt rather than re-charging. Use the order id as the gateway idempotency key so a retried charge can never double-bill; distinguish authorize, capture, void, and refund explicitly.

Payments can confirm asynchronously: an order may sit PENDING until a webhook arrives. Inbound webhooks are deduped in payment_webhook_events keyed by the gateway event id, so a redelivery is applied once. A reconciliation job sweeps stale PENDING_PAYMENT orders and incomplete attempts, reading gateway state to either complete (mark PAID) or compensate (release + FAILED).

Idempotency keys are retained for a bounded window (e.g. 24–72h); a repeat with the same key replays the stored result — including terminal failures stored with a null order_id — and a repeat with a different payload is rejected rather than silently re-run.

flows.name: Order is PENDING but payment fails -> compensate
flows.note: The durable PENDING_PAYMENT order exists before capture, so on payment failure the saga voids/refunds, releases the hold, and marks the order FAILED — no money or stock is stranded, and there is always a local record to reconcile.

traps.trap: Capturing payment before any durable order exists
traps.why: If the order write then fails, you have moved money with no local record — reconciliation and support are blind, and 'refund compensates' is not the same as never entering the bad state.
traps.instead: Create a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order (frozen price + reservation ids) before capture, so a charge always has a recoverable order to reconcile against.

traps.trap: No compensation when a later step fails
traps.why: A failed step strands the shopper's money and the reserved stock.
traps.instead: On failure, run compensations: void/refund the charge, release the reservation, and mark the order FAILED.

traps.why: A retried request re-reserves, re-charges, or re-orders.
traps.instead: Idempotency key: a repeat returns the original result without re-running steps; reuse the order id as the gateway key so the charge itself is idempotent.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Exactly one order and one charge.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotency key dedupes the second request to the same in-progress/completed result.

failureDrills.scenario: The gateway captures payment but the response is lost before the order is marked PAID.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The order eventually reaches PAID exactly once; no second charge.
failureDrills.mitigation: The order id keys the gateway charge, so a retry is deduped; an async webhook and a reconciliation sweep of stale PENDING orders complete the transition.

strong: Orchestrates with compensations and full idempotency.

recap.before: Multi-service checkout couldn't be atomic or safely retried.
recap.after: A compensating, idempotent saga gives all-or-nothing checkout, with a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order created before capture so money is never stranded without a recoverable record.
recap.newRisk: Saga + compensation logic is complex; every step must be idempotent, and a webhook/reconciler must close the gap between gateway state and order state.

whyNow: The saga ties inventory, payment, and orders into a correct checkout.

view.caption: The shopper app sends checkout plus an idempotency key to the Checkout Orchestrator, which dedupes via the Idempotency Store, then runs the saga in order: reserve inventory -> create a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order -> authorize/capture payment -> mark PAID and confirm the holds.

options.name: Idempotent orchestrated saga (default)
options.description: A central orchestrator drives reserve-inventory then create a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order then authorize/capture payment then mark PAID, deduping retries with a client idempotency key (using the order id as the gateway key) and running compensations (void/refund, release, mark FAILED) on failure. An asynchronous payment webhook reconciles any capture whose response was lost.
pros: A client-supplied idempotency key dedupes retries, so a dropped response never double-charges or double-reserves; the order id keys the gateway charge so the payment side is idempotent too.
The order exists (PENDING_PAYMENT) before money moves, so there is always a durable local record — capture is never stranded without a recoverable order.
Centralized state makes every partial-failure branch recoverable and auditable; a reconciliation job and payment webhook close any gap between gateway state and order state.
cons: Compensation is eventually consistent: a brief window where a capture has happened but the order is still PENDING — closed by the webhook/reconciler.
Requires an idempotency store (with a retention window) and careful per-step idempotency, plus payload-mismatch handling on key reuse.
The orchestrator concentrates logic and must itself be reliable.

view.caption: The Checkout Orchestrator dedupes the idempotency key against the Idempotency Store, then drives the saga in order: reserve at the Inventory Service -> create a PENDING_PAYMENT order at the Order Service -> authorize/capture at the Payment System -> mark PAID and confirm the holds.

options.name: Choreographed saga (events)
options.description: Inventory, Payment, and Order services react to each other's events to advance and compensate the checkout, with no central orchestrator.
pros: No central orchestrator — Inventory, Payment, and Order react to each other's events.
Looser coupling; each service owns its own step and compensation.
Services scale and deploy independently off the event stream.
cons: The end-to-end checkout flow is implicit and spread across subscribers — hard to trace.
No single place answers 'where is this checkout stuck' for support.
Event-ordering and cyclic-dependency bugs are easy to introduce.

view.caption: The Checkout Orchestrator emits CheckoutStarted to the Outbox, whose events drive the Inventory Service, Payment System, and Order Service with no central orchestrator.

options.name: Synchronous two-phase commit
options.description: Reserve, charge, and create-order are wrapped in a single distributed transaction that a coordinator prepares and commits all-or-nothing.
pros: All-or-nothing across reserve, charge, and order create in one atomic commit — no compensations.
No intermediate state where money is taken but no order exists.
Conceptually the cleanest correctness story.
cons: External payment systems don't speak 2PC, so it can't actually span the charge.
A prepare phase holds locks across services, crushing checkout throughput.
A coordinator crash mid-commit leaves participants blocked indefinitely.

view.caption: A 2PC coordinator prepares and commits the reserve, charge, and create across the Inventory Service, Payment System, and Order Service all-or-nothing.

steps.title: 6. Order State Machine & Fulfillment
steps.description: Model the order as a state machine — pending_payment -> paid -> fulfillment_requested -> fulfilled, with cancel_requested/cancelled and failed branches — and only allow legal transitions. Each transition is recorded in order_events and emits an outbox event in the same transaction; a relay publishes to a queue so idempotent consumers (keyed by event id) act exactly once. Cancellation compensates by current state: pending_payment releases holds, paid (not fulfilled) voids/refunds and releases, fulfilled becomes a return/refund flow rather than a simple cancel.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you reliably tell the warehouse to ship, exactly once, when an order is paid?

concepts.definition: Only legal state changes are allowed (e.g. can't fulfill a cancelled order).
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the order lifecycle consistent and auditable.

concepts.definition: Events written in the same transaction as the state change, relayed to a queue.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees a 'paid' event is published iff the state committed — no lost or phantom ships.

deepDives.title: Legal transitions and cancellation matrix
points: States: pending_payment -> paid -> fulfillment_requested -> fulfilled; pending_payment/paid can branch to cancel_requested -> cancelled; pending_payment can go to failed. Anything else is rejected.

Each transition writes an order_events row and an outbox_events row in the same transaction; the transition owner is the service that holds that state (checkout for pending->paid, fulfillment for ->fulfilled).

Cancel by current state: pending_payment releases holds and voids any auth; paid-but-not-fulfilled voids/refunds and releases; fulfilled is out of scope for cancel — it routes to a return/refund flow.

Consumers dedupe on event_id — each downstream service owns a small processed-events table keyed by event_id — so a redelivered order.paid ships once; guarded transitions stop a cancelled order from later being fulfilled or a fulfilled order from being silently cancelled.

options.name: Transactional outbox + queue (default)
options.description: Each guarded state transition writes its events to an outbox in the same transaction; a relay pushes them to a queue, so idempotent consumers get events exactly once relative to the change.
pros: Exactly-once-effect events relative to the state change.
Decouples fulfillment/notifications; consumers dedupe.
cons: Outbox relay + pruning to operate; consumers must be idempotent.

view.caption: Each Orders DB state change writes events to the Outbox in the same transaction, and a relay forwards them to the Order Event Queue.

options.name: Directly call fulfillment from checkout
options.description: Checkout writes the order state and then directly calls the fulfillment service in the same flow.
pros: Simplest; immediate.
cons: A dual-write/failure leaves order state and fulfillment inconsistent (ships with no order, or order with no ship).
Tight coupling.

view.caption: The Order Service writes the order state and then directly calls the Fulfillment Service in the same flow.

traps.trap: Calling fulfillment directly and writing order state separately
traps.why: A crash between the two ships without an order or orders without shipping — a dual-write hazard.
traps.instead: Write state + event in one transaction (outbox); relay to fulfillment, which dedupes.

traps.why: Cancelling a shipped order or fulfilling a cancelled one corrupts the lifecycle.

failureDrills.scenario: The fulfillment consumer redelivers a 'paid' event.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent fulfillment keyed by order id; the outbox guarantees the event matches committed state.

strong: Uses a guarded state machine + outbox; idempotent consumers.

recap.before: Order state and downstream actions could diverge.
recap.after: A guarded state machine emits reliable events via outbox to fulfillment.
recap.newRisk: Outbox relay and idempotent consumers are now required.

whyNow: A placed order must drive fulfillment reliably without dual-write bugs.

view.caption: The Order Service writes each state transition and its events to the Orders DB and Outbox, which a relay pushes to the Order Event Queue for the Fulfillment Service to consume.

flows.name: Transactional outbox -> fulfillment
flows.note: The state change and its event are written in one transaction; a relay publishes the outbox to the queue so there are no dual-write losses.

messages.label: tx: update state + insert outbox row

steps.title: 7. Scaling Carts vs Orders
steps.description: Scale the two workloads separately: carts (huge, read/write-heavy, availability-first) shard across a fast KV store; orders (fewer, correctness-critical) stay on a consistent, replicated store. A router directs cart traffic by user; orders scale by partitioning on order/user id.
steps.decisionPrompt: Carts get 25x the traffic of checkouts but need weaker guarantees. Why scale them differently?

concepts.definition: Carts and orders are different systems with opposite priorities (AP vs CP).
concepts.whyItMatters: One datastore/consistency model can't serve both well.

concepts.definition: Partition cart and order data by a key that keeps a user's data together.
concepts.whyItMatters: Scales horizontally while keeping a user's cart/orders local.

options.name: Sharded AP cart store + consistent order DB (default)
options.description: Carts shard across a fast availability-first KV store while orders stay on a strongly-consistent replicated database, with checkout re-validating at the boundary between them.
pros: Each workload scales on its own terms; cart volume can't threaten order correctness.
Cart KV shards by user; orders stay strongly consistent.
cons: Two systems to operate; checkout bridges them (re-validating at the boundary).

view.caption: The Shard Router spreads carts across the fast availability-first Cart Shard A and Cart Shard B.

options.name: One shared SQL DB for carts and orders
options.description: Both carts and orders live in a single strongly-consistent SQL database.
pros: Simplest; one system, strong consistency everywhere.
cons: Cart write volume contends with and can starve correctness-critical order/inventory work.
Single scaling ceiling.

view.caption: Both carts and orders are served by the Commerce API from a single shared Orders DB.

deepDives.title: Bridging the two stores
points: Cart is intent (AP); at checkout, re-read authoritative price and reserve authoritative stock (CP).
Shard carts by user for locality; expire abandoned carts to bound storage.

Sharding the cart store does nothing for a hot SKU — that contention lands on one inventory row and is handled at the inventory layer (CAS holds, then admission control or sharded reservation buckets from Step 4), not by cart sharding.

traps.trap: Forcing carts and orders into one consistency model
traps.why: Strong consistency for carts wastes availability/scale; weak consistency for orders risks money/inventory errors.
traps.instead: Split them: AP cart store, CP order/inventory store; reconcile at checkout.

traps.trap: Sharding orders by something other than order/user
traps.why: Scatters a user's orders and complicates lookups and the saga.
traps.instead: Partition by order/user id to keep related data and the saga local.

strong: Separates AP carts from CP orders and shards each appropriately.
Bridges them with re-validation at checkout.
weak: One shared DB and consistency model.
Lets cart load threaten order correctness.

recap.before: One system tried to serve both workloads.
recap.after: Carts and orders scale independently with fitting consistency models.
recap.newRisk: Two systems to operate; the checkout boundary must re-validate.

whyNow: The two workloads have opposite needs — separating them is the key scaling decision.

view.caption: The Commerce API routes cart ops through the Shard Router, which partitions by user across Cart Shard A and B, while orders go straight to the Orders DB.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: carts live in a sharded, available, expiring KV store (intent only) behind the Cart Service; a pricing service computes promotions, re-validated and frozen at checkout; checkout runs as an idempotent saga that reserves inventory via CAS-protected TTL holds, creates a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order, charges payment (order id as the gateway key), then marks it PAID — compensating on any failure and reconciling via webhook; the order is a guarded state machine recording transitions and emitting events via a transactional outbox to fulfillment; carts (AP) and orders/inventory (CP) scale as separate systems.

view.caption: The shopper app hits the Commerce API, which routes cart ops to the Cart Service; the Cart Service shards carts via the Shard Router (Cart Shards A/B), and Pricing & Promotions prices them; the Checkout Orchestrator dedupes against the Idempotency Store, reserves inventory, creates the order, charges payment, and marks it paid, whose state changes flow through the Outbox and Order Event Queue to the Fulfillment Service.

functional.how: Cart service over a fast KV store.
Durable cart store keyed by user; guest merge on login.
Pricing service + rule engine, frozen at checkout.
Idempotent saga: reserve -> create PENDING_PAYMENT order -> authorize/capture -> mark PAID and confirm holds.
Guarded order state machine with compensations.

nonFunctional.requirement: Never oversell / no charge without a recoverable order
nonFunctional.how: CAS-protected TTL reservations; a durable PENDING_PAYMENT order created before capture; compensating saga with webhook/reconciler.
Idempotency key dedupes retries to one order/charge.
Sharded AP KV store separate from orders.
Consistent order DB + saga + outbox events.
Server-side re-evaluation and price freeze at checkout.
AP cart shards + CP order/inventory store.

say: Restate it: a durable cart and a correct, multi-service checkout (payment + inventory + order).
List functional (cart CRUD, pricing, checkout, lifecycle) and non-functional (no oversell, idempotent checkout, cart availability, order durability).
Name the split: carts are availability-first; orders/inventory are correctness-first.
Note cart ops dwarf checkouts but need weaker guarantees.
Sketch cart item PUT, cart GET, idempotent checkout, and cancel.
Start naive to expose the distributed-transaction problem, then a persistent cart store, then pricing.
Add inventory reservation/CAS, then the idempotent checkout saga, then the order state machine + outbox.
Go deep where pushed: the saga + compensations, or oversell prevention.
Scale carts (AP, sharded) and orders (CP) separately; bridge at checkout.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant risk (cross-service checkout correctness).

expectations: Persists the cart instead of keeping it in memory.
Knows checkout must reserve stock and take payment, and that retries shouldn't double-charge.
Designs an idempotent checkout saga with compensations across services.
Prevents oversell with atomic CAS reservations + TTL.
Re-validates and freezes price server-side at checkout.
Splits AP carts from CP orders/inventory and scales each appropriately.
Uses a guarded order state machine + transactional outbox for reliable fulfillment.
Reasons through every partial-failure branch and its compensation.

followUps: How would you handle partial fulfillment and split shipments from multiple warehouses?
How do you support returns and refunds that reverse inventory and payment after fulfillment?
How would you implement a flash sale where one SKU sees extreme checkout contention?
How do you reconcile inventory across the reservation store, orders, and the warehouse system of record?
How would you support saved carts, wishlists, and 'buy now' (skip-cart) flows?
How do you prevent and detect payment fraud during checkout without hurting conversion?
